title: How chronic pain syndromes relate to unresolved emotional material
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your body isn't betraying you. It's talking to you. 

 I spent years believing my chronic pain was just bad luck. A random glitch in my nervous system. So...
htmlContent:
Your body isn't betraying you. It's talking to you.

I spent years believing my chronic pain was just bad luck. A random glitch in my nervous system. Something I'd have to manage forever with heating pads and ibuprofen and gritted teeth. The doctors told me my scans were clean. My bloodwork was normal. "We can't find anything wrong," they'd say, shrugging. And I'd walk out of their offices feeling crazy. Feeling like maybe I was making it up. Maybe the pain was all in my head.

Here's the thing. They were right about one thing. It was in my head. But not the way they meant.

It was in my brain. In my nervous system. In the parts of me that learned, years before I could form sentences, that the world wasn't safe. That love came with conditions. That my body wasn't mine to protect.

Chronic pain isn't just a physical problem. It's a storage problem. Your body holds onto experiences your mind couldn't process. And when the emotional load gets too heavy, your body finds a way to make you feel it. Literally.

I'm not talking about metaphor here. I'm talking about real, measurable biology. Your nervous system doesn't distinguish between a physical threat and an emotional one. A car accident and a childhood of neglect both activate the same survival circuits. And when those circuits stay stuck in the "on" position, your body starts sending distress signals. Pain is one of those signals.

Think about it. You can't cry in a meeting. You can't scream at your boss. You can't run from your own memories. So your body does the only thing it can. It turns down the volume on your life by turning up the volume on your pain.

Wild, right? Your own body, working overtime to protect you from something that already happened.

Let me ask you something. When was the last time you felt truly relaxed in your body? Not distracted. Not numbed out. Actually relaxed. If you can't remember, that tells you something important. Your nervous system may have forgotten what rest feels like. It may have decided, somewhere along the way, that rest is dangerous.

Deb Dana talks about this - how the dorsal vagal state can look like relaxation from the outside but actually feels like collapse on the inside. There's a difference between choosing to rest and being unable to move. There's a difference between peace and shutdown. Your body knows the difference, even if the people around you can't tell.

Notice your hands right now. Are they clenched? Open? Cold? Warm? Your hands tell a story about your autonomic state. Cold hands often mean sympathetic activation - blood has moved to the core, preparing for threat. Warm, relaxed hands suggest ventral vagal engagement - safety. It's a small thing to notice. But small things are where healing lives.

The science of stored emotion

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. His research at the Trauma Center in Boston showed that people with unresolved trauma have measurable differences in their brain activity, their stress hormones, even their immune function. He wrote about this in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), a book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful. And what he found was simple and devastating: your body remembers what your mind tries to forget.

Then there's Dr. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing. He noticed that animals in the wild don't get traumatized. They shake off their stress responses and move on. But humans? We suppress. We freeze. We hold our breath and clench our jaws and tell ourselves we're fine. And that unprocessed survival energy gets stored in our tissues. It becomes tension. It becomes inflammation. It becomes chronic pain.

I remember the first time I understood this intellectually. I was sitting in my therapist's office, and she asked me where I felt my anger. I pointed to my lower back. "Right there," I said. "That knot that's been there for seven years." She didn't say much. Just nodded. But something clicked for me in that moment. That knot wasn't just a muscle spasm. It was a story my body had been telling me for almost a decade. A story about being small. About being powerless. About swallowing my rage because expressing it wasn't safe.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying every case of chronic back pain is caused by unexpressed anger. I'm not saying you can think your way out of fibromyalgia or migraines or IBS. What I'm saying is that the line between emotional and physical pain is much thinner than we've been taught. And ignoring that connection means leaving a huge part of your healing on the table.

How emotional material becomes physical sensation

Your nervous system has a job. It scans for danger. It activates your stress response when it finds something threatening. And it deactivates that response when the threat passes. That's the cycle. That's how it's supposed to work.

But here's where things go wrong. When you experience something overwhelming, especially as a child, your nervous system can get stuck in the activation phase. The threat never really passes. Not in your body's memory. So your muscles stay tight. Your breathing stays shallow. Your immune system stays on high alert. And over time, that constant activation wears down your tissues. It creates inflammation. It disrupts sleep. It makes your nerves hypersensitive.

You start feeling pain from stimuli that shouldn't hurt. A light touch becomes unbearable. A normal day leaves you exhausted. Your body is screaming at you, but the message is garbled. It's saying "danger" when you're just sitting at your desk. It's saying "fight or flight" when you're trying to fall asleep.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium helps regulate the nervous system and can reduce muscle tension. It's not a cure, but it can take the edge off while you do the deeper work.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

I've also found that managing sensory input helps. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower the volume of the world without blocking sound completely. For someone whose nervous system is already overloaded, that can make a real difference.

The point is this: your pain isn't random. It's connected to something. And finding that connection can be the first step toward real relief.

The emotional roots of specific pain patterns

I want to be careful here. I'm not offering a map. Every body is different. But certain patterns show up again and again in clinical work.

Chronic neck and shoulder tension often correlates with carrying too much responsibility. With feeling like you have to hold everything together. With the weight of other people's expectations pressing down on you. I had a client who described her shoulder pain as "carrying the world on my back." She wasn't being poetic. She was describing exactly what her body was doing.

Lower back pain frequently connects to fear. Fear of falling. Fear of failing. Fear of not being supported. The lower back is your foundation. When that foundation feels shaky, your muscles tighten to stabilize you. But if the perceived threat is emotional, not physical, that tightness never releases.

Digestive issues are almost always linked to the nervous system. Your gut is lined with neurons. It's your second brain. And when your first brain is stuck in survival mode, your second brain goes into lockdown. Digestion shuts down. Inflammation increases. You develop sensitivities to foods that were never a problem before.

Headaches and migraines often connect to self-criticism. To the inner voice that never stops judging. To the pressure you put on yourself to be perfect. Your brain literally hurts from trying to control everything.

I'm not saying you can trace every symptom back to a specific childhood memory. That's reductive and it's not how this works. But I am saying that if you've been to every doctor, tried every treatment, and nothing has changed, it's worth asking: what is this pain protecting me from feeling?

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

Because that's what chronic pain often is. A protection. A way to avoid something that feels even worse. Like grief. Or rage. Or the terrifying realization that you've been living your life for other people.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It explains how trauma gets trapped in the body and offers practical ways to release it.

Where do you go from here?

This is the hard part. Because I can't give you a five-step plan to cure your chronic pain. Anyone who promises that is selling something. The truth is messier. The truth is that healing from chronic pain often means healing from your entire life. And that takes time. And patience. And a willingness to feel things you've been avoiding for years.

But here's what I can tell you. Start paying attention to the connection between your emotions and your body. Notice what happens when you feel angry. Where do you feel it? What happens when you feel sad? When you feel scared? Just notice. Don't try to change anything. Don't judge yourself for having these responses. Just observe.

If you can, find a therapist who understands trauma and the body. Somatic Experiencing practitioners. EMDR therapists. Internal Family Systems therapists. People who know that talk alone isn't enough. People who will help you befriend your body instead of fighting it.

And please, be gentle with yourself. You didn't choose this pain. It's not your fault. Your body did the best it could with what it had. And it's still trying to protect you, even if that protection has become a prison.

Sit with that for a moment. Your pain is not your enemy. It's a messenger. And if you can learn to listen to it, without judgment, without fear, you might just find your way home.

Your body is not broken. It's burdened. And burdens can be laid down.
